name: Christmas Cat Rescue
description: A heartwarming Christmas story about a brave cat saving its owner from a fire
petDescriptionOriginal: the same small fluffy orange tabby cat with soft orange-and-cream striped fur, big expressive eyes, a tiny pink nose,
framePromptTemplate: {PET_DESCRIPTION} wearing a red Christmas knitted sweater with a tiny jingle bell collar, playing near a 13-year-old American boy with short dark hair in a white hoodie and dark jogger pants, Christmas Eve night in a modern apartment, decorated Christmas tree beside a large window with light snow falling outside, soft golden fairy lights glowing, wrapped gifts and a bright red yarn ball on a fluffy rug, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, cinematic wide establishing shot, 16:9 aspect ratio, first frame for animation sequence

shots.Scene:
Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, always the same stylized Pixar-like world. {PET_DESCRIPTION} wearing a red Christmas knitted sweater with a tiny jingle bell collar, and the same 13-year-old American boy with short dark hair, wearing a white hoodie and comfortable dark jogger pants. Fully stylized 3D characters with soft rounded shapes and appealing proportions, consistent appearance across all shots, hero cat tone,
a wide handheld establishing shot of a cozy modern living room. On the left, a decorated Christmas tree stands beside a large window with light snow falling outside, soft golden fairy lights glowing. Wrapped gifts and a bright red yarn ball sit on a fluffy rug at the base of the tree. On the right, a soft fabric sofa faces slightly toward camera. The 13-year-old boy is already lying sideways on the sofa, wearing his white hoodie and dark joggers, oversized gaming headphones on his head, actively playing a video game on a glowing laptop resting on his lap; fast game colors flicker on his face and headphones.
On the floor near the tree, the same pet in its red sweater is happily playing by itself with the red yarn ball, batting at it with soft paws, occasionally pouncing in a cute, slightly exaggerated Pixar-style motion. The cat is not asking for attention yet; the boy is lost in his game, and there is no smoke, no fire, no danger in sight.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, always the same stylized Pixar-like world. {PET_DESCRIPTION} and the same 13-year-old boy, consistent appearance across shots, hero cat tone,
a medium handheld shot at cat level near the sofa. The red yarn ball rolls a little away as the cat gives it one last bat, then loses interest and turns toward the boy. The cat pads over to the sofa and stands up with its front paws on the side cushion, big eyes shining, giving a few soft meows, its tiny bell jingling. It gently bumps its head against the boy's leg in a clear "play with me" gesture. The boy glances down for a moment but keeps his eyes mostly on the laptop and says in a distracted tone, "Later, okay?" — the only line of dialogue in this shot. He nudges the cat back down toward the rug with his foot, still focused on the game.
In the same continuous shot, the camera eases slightly toward a side table near the tree where his free hand reaches almost automatically: he turns a small space heater up to maximum and plugs an extra string of old Christmas lights into an already crowded power strip partly hidden behind the tall Christmas tree and curtain. We clearly see a messy tangle of plugs and cables, but there is still no visible smoke, no flame yet — just a visual hint of danger that the boy ignores.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy design, consistent, hero cat tone,
a close-up at floor level behind the Christmas tree in a steady, controlled shot. The camera slowly pushes in on the overloaded power strip jammed with plugs and old Christmas lights. One of the plugs glows faintly orange. A tiny spark jumps, and a small ember appears where the sheer curtain brushes the plug. A thin, very local thread of gray smoke begins to rise, staying right behind the tree and close to the wall. From the living room side, the tree, sofa back, and curtain completely block this view; neither the boy nor the cat can directly see it yet. The only sound is distant game audio and the soft hum of the heater.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy, consistent, hero cat tone,
a medium low-angle handheld shot from a corner of the living room, showing both the rug and part of the sofa. The cat lies on the rug near the abandoned red yarn ball, partly facing the Christmas tree. As the camera holds, a faint orange glow begins to appear behind the branches, reflecting softly on a small patch of wall near the floor — a glow that is blocked from the boy's view by the tall sofa back and the tree, but visible to the camera and the cat. The thin smoke has risen closer to the ceiling in the back corner, still far above the boy's normal eye level and behind him.
The cat's ears twitch sharply; it lifts its head, sniffs the air, eyes widening in a clear Pixar-style "something is wrong" expression, tail puffing slightly. In the blurred background, we see the boy on the sofa from behind; his shoulders and the sofa back are turned toward the tree and the back corner, headphones still on, his face pointed toward the laptop on the opposite side of the room. From his position, there is still no obvious smoke or flame in his line of sight, which explains why he hasn't noticed yet.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy, consistent, hero cat tone,
a handheld medium shot at cat level as the cat rushes toward the sofa. The camera tracks with it. The cat jumps up, front paws landing on the edge of the couch, meowing more urgently now, tapping the boy's arm with quick repeated paw swipes, its bell jingling fast. The boy frowns slightly and pulls one ear cup of his headphones off so he can hear better, but he still does not turn his head around; his back remains toward the tree and the small fire, and the thin smoke high in the corner is still above his peripheral vision.
With a classic teenage sigh, he says, "Not now, I'm almost winning," the only line in this shot. His mouth clearly forms the words. He slides the cat gently back down toward the rug with his forearm and puts the headphone cup back on. From his point of view, the room still looks normal in front of him — only the audience and the cat know there is a hidden problem behind.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy, consistent, hero cat tone,
a dynamic handheld shot begins from the cat's side near the tree and then swings toward the sofa in one continuous move. At the start of the shot, we briefly see the sheer curtain behind the tree where the tiny ember has just become a small, narrow flame licking up a few inches of fabric. The fire is still mostly hidden behind the tree and curtain edge; it is real but limited and partly blocked. A bit more gray smoke curls along the very top corner of the room, still concentrated near the ceiling above the tree, not yet filling the main space.
In the same shot, the camera whips toward the sofa as the cat leaps onto the coffee table, skids, and knocks over a drink in a slightly comedic splash. Without stopping, it jumps to the boy's side and bites firmly into the fabric of his white hoodie sleeve, bracing its back paws on the cushion and tugging with its whole body. The boy jolts, finally tearing his eyes from the screen, pausing the game, and sitting up. He coughs once from the subtle change in air and looks confused, but in this instant he still has not turned far enough to see the fire directly — that will happen in the next shot, almost immediately.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy, consistent, hero cat tone,
a tight handheld close-up on the boy's Pixar-style face as he starts to turn his head toward the tree, pulled by the cat on his sleeve. In the same continuous shot, the smoke alarm suddenly shrieks overhead and a harsh white strobe flashes from above, reflecting in his eyes and on the glossy parts of his headphones now around his neck. A thicker wave of gray smoke rolls across the very top of frame.
As he completes the turn, the camera shifts to an over-the-shoulder angle without cutting, revealing for the first clear time from his point of view the burning curtain and part of the Christmas tree: small but real stylized flames, and smoky orange light in the background. This is the first moment the audience sees the fire from the boy's perspective. His eyes widen; his expression snaps from confusion to real fear. He gasps softly and says just one short line, "Oh no…", the only piece of dialogue in this shot. The cat is still attached to his sleeve, pulling him away from the flames.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, always the same stylized Pixar-like world. {PET_DESCRIPTION} wearing a red Christmas knitted sweater with a tiny jingle bell collar, and the same 13-year-old American boy with short dark hair, wearing a white hoodie and comfortable dark jogger pants. Fully stylized 3D characters with soft rounded shapes and appealing proportions, consistent appearance across all shots, hero cat tone,
a smoky handheld medium shot in the hallway, one continuous tracking move. The camera moves backward as the boy stumbles out of the living room toward the front door, now fully alarmed after seeing the fire. His headphones are off his ears and hang loosely around his neck. One arm is bent so the crook of his elbow covers his nose and mouth, the other arm is slightly outstretched for balance. His eyes are wide and watering, but the smoke in the hallway is still at a moderate, believable level: a hazy layer hanging higher up near the ceiling, not yet filling the whole corridor.
Just ahead of him, the same pet trots with urgent little steps, teeth gripping the same sleeve of his white hoodie, tugging him forward toward the door. The cat glances back at him repeatedly, its bell jingling in a fast, rhythmic way. In the same shot, the camera briefly tilts down to show the boy's socked feet and the cat's paws sliding slightly on the floor as they move, then tilts back up to his scared but determined face. Through the open doorway behind them, we can still see into the living room: stylized flames on the curtain and part of the tree, and thicker smoke gathering near the ceiling, clearly showing that staying inside is now dangerous and leaving is the right choice.

Christmas Eve night in the same modern apartment, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, always the same stylized Pixar-like world. {PET_DESCRIPTION} and the same 13-year-old boy, consistent appearance across shots, hero cat tone,
a low-angle medium shot down the smoky hallway toward the open front door, handheld but controlled. The camera faces the doorway as the boy stumbles out into the clearer building corridor, coughing, then is gently pulled farther away by a concerned neighbor or firefighter who guides him toward the stairwell. The hallway outside the apartment is hazy but not full of smoke.
The same pet reaches the threshold right behind him and then skids to an abrupt stop, claws scraping lightly on the floor in a stylized, non-violent way. In this same shot, the boy turns back once and calls out, "Milo! Come on!" (or another short cat name), but a firefighter's arm lightly blocks his chest, stopping him from re-entering. His voice cracks with worry, making it clear he does not want to leave the cat behind.
The camera dollies in to a close-up on the cat standing at the door line. From here, we see two directions: in front of it, the boy being held back in the corridor; behind it, just inside the open apartment, an entry console table a few steps inside the door with a framed family photo on top. In the photo, a younger version of the boy holds the same cat as a tiny kitten, both smiling. Flickering firelight reflects on the glass. Smoke drifts in the background, but the visible flames are still further back in the living room, not yet reaching the console near the door.
The cat's ears fold back; its eyes narrow with determination in a very Pixar-style "hero decision" moment. Without cutting, the camera pans and tracks with the cat as it spins around and darts just a short distance back into the apartment toward the console, bell jingling sharply. The shot ends as the cat jumps up with its front paws on the cabinet, stretches its neck, and bites down firmly on the photo frame, lifting it clearly into view. The danger is real but time is very short, and we understand instantly: the cat refuses to leave without saving this memory.

Christmas Eve night outside the same apartment building, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same boy and cat designs, consistent, hero cat tone,
a cinematic crane shot begins wide on the outside of the apartment building. Light snow falls through flashing red and blue emergency lights. A stylized fire truck is parked on the street, firefighters move with calm urgency, and neighbors gather in small groups as Pixar-style background characters. The camera glides downward toward the sidewalk.
It settles into a medium close shot of the boy sitting on the curb, wrapped in a metallic emergency blanket that reflects the colorful lights. His white hoodie is smudged with ash, his hair is messy, and his cheeks are flushed from heat and cold. In his hands, he tightly holds the small red cat collar with its broken clip. His fingers squeeze it so hard his knuckles go pale. He keeps looking toward the building entrance and the smoky windows above, searching for any sign of his cat.
A firefighter in the background briefly speaks with another firefighter and shakes their head, and the boy misreads this as bad news. He looks down, shoulders slumping, eyes glassy and full of tears. One silent tear rolls down his cheek as he presses the collar to his chest and curls forward around it. In his mind, the cat ran back inside and did not come out. There is still no cheering, only distant sirens, the soft rumble of the fire truck, and hushed, sympathetic murmurs from neighbors around him.

Christmas Eve night outside the same apartment building, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, same cat and boy designs, consistent, hero cat tone,
a medium handheld shot frames the building entrance. The doorway is still hazy with smoke, but clearly more under control now. In the center of the frame, a firefighter in full stylized gear steps out of the doorway, carefully cradling the sooty but lively pet in their arms. The cat's fur is smudged with gray ash, but its big eyes are bright and alert. It is still holding the framed family photo firmly in its mouth, and the tiny bell on its collar jingles with every step.
The camera slowly pushes in as the firefighter walks forward. In the background, we see neighbors begin to turn and notice; some raise their hands to point. The shot pans to reveal the boy, still sitting on the curb, looking down at the collar in his hands. As he hears the bell and the rising murmur of voices, he looks up, eyes widening in disbelief.
The firefighter approaches and kneels slightly so the boy and the cat are at the same level. Gently, they transfer the cat into the boy's arms. The boy hugs the cat tightly to his chest, the smoke-stained photo frame pressed between them, the red collar still wrapped in his fist. Around them, neighbors and firefighters start clapping and cheering, some smiling, some wiping away tears, many pointing at the cat like it's a tiny movie hero. Over the wave of applause and the soft rumble of the fire truck, we clearly hear the firefighter say warmly, "This little one's a real hero." It is the only spoken line in this shot. The cat's bell jingles proudly as it pushes its head under the boy's chin and closes its eyes for a brief, contented blink.

Later the same Christmas Eve night, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, always the same stylized Pixar-like world. {PET_DESCRIPTION} wearing the same red Christmas knitted sweater with a tiny jingle bell collar and a new shiny hero medal, and the same 13-year-old American boy with short dark hair, wearing the same white hoodie and comfortable dark jogger pants. Fully stylized 3D characters with soft rounded shapes and appealing proportions, consistent appearance across all shots, hero cat tone,
a calm medium-wide shot inside a simpler, cozy temporary living room, like a relative's small apartment. The camera is gently drifting, almost still, giving a feeling of safety after the chaos. A modest tabletop Christmas tree with a few simple ornaments sits on a side table near a warm lamp, casting a soft golden glow over the room.
In the background, a TV on a low stand is playing a local news segment. On the screen we see clear on-screen title text in big white letters: "HERO CAT SAVES BOY ON CHRISTMAS EVE." Beneath the title, the TV shows a photo taken earlier that evening: the same boy in his white hoodie and the same pet in its red sweater, both a little shy but smiling toward the camera, the hero medal visible on the cat's collar. For a moment, the TV shows quick clips of the fire truck, the crowd outside, and the firefighter holding the cat — stylized versions of what we saw before — then returns to the still photo.
In the foreground, in sharp focus, the real pet sits upright on a small sofa facing the TV, wearing its red sweater, bell, and shiny hero medal. Its tail is wrapped neatly around its paws, posture calm but proud, ears slightly perked as if listening to its own story. Right beside it on the same sofa sits the boy, still in his white hoodie and dark joggers, no longer wrapped in the emergency blanket. He is sitting close enough that his shoulder gently leans against the cat's side; one hand rests softly on the cat's back, fingers curling slightly into the sweater fabric.
The boy watches the TV with a soft, private smile, eyes shining with a mix of relief, gratitude, and amazement. Every few seconds he glances down at the cat, as if still checking that it is really there. On a small coffee table in front of them, slightly out of focus, we can see the rescued family photo frame resting safely beside a small folded paper or award card, both catching the warm light — a quiet reminder of what the cat chose to save. The sound is low and gentle: the calm voice of the news anchor talking about a "brave cat," a faint echo of earlier applause in the news clip, and soft indoor room tone. There are no sirens anymore, no shouting, only a peaceful sense that they are safe together and silently proud of what the little cat has done.

Same night, in the same temporary home, Pixar-style 3D animated feature film look, full CG animation, not live-action, not photorealistic, {PET_DESCRIPTION} with its red Christmas sweater, bell and hero medal, and the same 13-year-old boy in his white hoodie and dark joggers, consistent design, hero cat tone,
an intimate close-up shows the boy sitting on a small sofa next to the little Christmas tree. The room is dim except for the warm lights from the tree and a nearby lamp. The camera starts close on his arms as he cradles the sleeping pet against his chest. The cat is curled up, eyes closed, breathing slow and peaceful. Its red sweater, tiny bell, and shiny hero medal rest clearly against the white fabric of the boy's hoodie. One of the boy's hands gently strokes the cat's back in a slow, grateful motion, while the other hand lightly holds the edge of the smoke-stained family photo frame resting on his lap.
In the soft-focus foreground on the coffee table, we see the same rescued family photo frame set down beside a small award card or certificate, both catching the warm light. As the camera very slowly pulls back to a soft wide shot, golden Christmas bokeh fills the background, keeping the boy and cat in sharp focus, unmistakably Pixar-style with soft rounded shapes and gentle, harmonious colors.
As the camera settles, large centered English text fades in over the lower half of the frame in warm, elegant typography:
"It was you who protected me, my little hero."
The boy's lips move in a quiet whisper that matches the words on screen, but his voice is almost inaudible, like a message spoken just for the cat. The cat shifts slightly in its sleep, the bell and medal giving a faint jingle, then settles again. The image holds for a brief, calm moment, ending on a warm, emotional note that makes every viewer who loves a cat imagine their own pet in the boy's arms as their "little hero."
